1: The ordinances (mishpatim) — the case law of the Book of the Covenant (Exodus 20:22-23:33) that applies the Decalogue's principles to specific situations. Jesus fulfills and deepens this legal tradition in the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:17-48) and in his summary of the law (Matthew 22:37-40). He came not to abolish the law but to fulfill it — filling it with its deepest intent and bearing its full obligation on behalf of his people (Galatians 3:13; Romans 8:3-4).
2: The Hebrew slave law — six years of service, freedom in the seventh. The sabbatical structure applied to human servitude. Christ announced fulfillment of this release-structure in Luke 4:18-19 (citing Isaiah 61:1): “He has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives.” He is the one who brings the ultimate jubilee: release from slavery to sin and death (Romans 6:17-18; Galatians 5:1: “It is for freedom that Christ has set us free”).
3: The slave who came single leaves single; the slave who came married leaves with his wife. The law protects the existing family unit when freedom comes. The principle of protecting family relationships in the act of liberation is the type of Christ's redemption, which does not dissolve but hallows existing covenant relationships (1 Corinthians 7:20-24).
4: If the master gave the slave a wife during service, the wife and children belong to the master at freedom. The complexity of this provision points to the legal framework designed to prevent exploitation: the slave cannot acquire a free-born household through the master's gift and then depart with it. The provision prioritizes clear legal status over sentiment — a principle governing the laws of property and persons throughout the Book of the Covenant.
5: The slave who loves his master and refuses freedom — “I love my master and my wife and children and do not want to go free.” Psalms 40:6-8 applies this image to Christ: “Ears you have opened for me” (LXX: “a body you have prepared for me”) — cited in Hebrews 10:5-7 as Christ's words at the Incarnation: “I have come to do your will, O God.” Christ is the eternal servant-Son who voluntarily entered servitude for his master (the Father) and his household (his people), choosing bondage out of love.
6: “His master must take him before the judges and bring him to the door or doorpost. There his master shall pierce his ear with an awl.” The ear-piercing at the doorpost is the voluntary-servitude sign. Psalms 40:6's “ears you have opened (karitha, bored/dug)” — applied in Hebrews 10:5-7 to Christ's incarnational commitment — is likely an allusion to this passage. The opened ear is the sign of the one who chose to remain in service out of love rather than claiming his rights.
7: The female slave sold by her father — she is not released on the same terms as a male slave. The law provides for her protection (Exodus 21:7-11) rather than simply granting her the same release. The differentiated provision reflects the legal complexity of the ANE household; its direction is protective rather than subordinating. Christ's ministry showed particular care for women who were legally vulnerable (Luke 7:36-50; Exodus 8:2-3; John 4:1-26; Exodus 8:3-11).
8: If the master who selected her does not find her pleasing, he must allow her redemption — he cannot sell her to foreigners, having dealt unfaithfully with her. The prohibition on betrayal-sale protects the enslaved woman from being trafficked. The law's direction is consistently toward protecting the vulnerable from exploitation. Christ's own redemption of the church is specifically called by the same term — ga'al, redeeem — YHWH who redeems those who have been “dealt with unfaithfully” by sin (Galatians 3:13; Revelation 5:9).
9: If the master selects her for his son, he must treat her as he would a daughter. The adoption-through-marriage principle: belonging to the son's household elevates the enslaved woman's status to daughter. This is the type of the believer's adoption through union with the Son: “you received the Spirit of adoption as sons” (Romans 8:15); belonging to Christ brings sonship/daughtership in the Father's household.
10: The master who marries another must not reduce the first woman's food, clothing, or marital rights. The protection of the first wife's rights preserves the covenant obligations of the first union. Christ as husband of the church is the faithful covenant-keeper who withholds nothing good from his bride (Ephesians 5:25-29: “he who loves his wife loves himself”).
11: If the master fails to provide these three things, the enslaved woman goes free without paying anything. Release without payment — freedom at no cost to the one released — is the structure of grace. Christ's redemption of his people costs them nothing: “you were bought at a price” (1 Corinthians 6:20), but the price was paid by him, not by those redeemed (Isaiah 52:3; 1 Peter 1:18-19).
12: Anyone who strikes and kills another must be put to death. The lex talionis principle applied to capital cases: life for life. Christ bore this death-penalty not for his own violence but for ours: “he was pierced for our transgressions” (Isaiah 53:5). The very law that condemns the murderer pronounces Christ's death — he who knew no sin was made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21), bearing the capital penalty of the law's violation.
13: Unintentional killing — a city of refuge is provided. “God allowed it to happen” — the distinction between intentional and unintentional reflects a theology of providence underlying the law. Christ is the ultimate city of refuge (Hebrews 6:18: “we who have fled to take hold of the hope set before us”) — the one to whom the guilty flee and find an asylum that not even the avenger of blood can penetrate.
14: Deliberate, scheming murder — even from YHWH's altar the killer is to be taken and executed. The altar provides no asylum for premeditated murder. This shows that the law's mercy (city of refuge) and justice (execution for premeditated murder) are both genuine. Christ bore the punishment of the deliberate, premeditated sinner while providing the refuge the law denied — not by reducing the penalty but by taking it himself (1 Peter 3:18).
15: Anyone who strikes their father or mother must be put to death. The severity of the penalty reflects the fifth commandment's centrality: parental authority is the foundation of all human social order. Jesus fulfilled this commandment while also setting it in its proper subordinate position to the first commandment (Matthew 10:37; Luke 14:26). His honor of Joseph and Mary (Luke 2:51; John 19:26-27) models the commandment's fulfillment.
16: Kidnapping — stealing a person and selling them or being caught with them — is a capital offense. The image of God in every person makes their theft equal to murder in gravity. Christ's redemption of enslaved humanity (Galatians 3:13; Colossians 1:13: “he has rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son he loves”) is the positive counterpart: he is the one who liberates the stolen and returns them to their rightful owner (the Father).
17: Anyone who curses their father or mother must be put to death. The severity parallels v15: the foundation of the covenant community in the household requires protection of parental authority from within as well as without. Jesus came not to abolish this commandment but to fulfill it — his own honor of his Father (John 8:49: “I honor my Father”) is the perfect fulfillment of the law he upholds.
18: The quarreling-and-injury law — one person hits another with a stone or fist; the injured person is bedridden but recovers. The law's graduated response: if recovery occurs, the striker pays for lost time and medical care but is not executed. Christ's own body was struck (Matthew 26:67; Exodus 27:30; Isaiah 50:6: “I offered my back to those who struck me”) — he is the innocent one who received the injury the law was designed to adjudicate.
19: If the injured person recovers and walks around, the striker is not held responsible for death but must compensate for the convalescence. The law distinguishes between intention, outcome, and compensation: it does not treat all violence as equivalent but calibrates the response to the actual harm. The same principle governs divine justice: Christ's atonement is not a blanket payment but a precisely calibrated satisfaction of every specific debt (Romans 3:25-26: “he did this to demonstrate his justice”).
20: A master who beats a slave to death shall be punished — the slave has rights before YHWH's law. The protection of the slave from lethal violence is a fundamental limitation on the master's authority. Christ's own incarnation as a slave (Philippians 2:7: morphen doulou, “the form of a servant/slave”) dignifies the slave's legal status: the Son of God took the form of the most protected-by-law person in the Book of the Covenant.
21: If the slave survives a day or two, no punishment — the slave is the master's property. The provision shows the law working within the ANE institution of slavery while limiting its abuses. The direction of the law is consistently toward protecting life, even of the legally owned. Christ's death as a slave (Philippians 2:8: “becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross”) is the ultimate inversion: the master of all becomes the slave who is legally “the master's property.”
22: Fighting men injure a pregnant woman causing premature birth — if no serious injury, a fine is paid. The law protects the unborn through the principle of compensation for harm to the mother. The protection of life at every stage — even prenatal — reflects the image-of-God principle underlying the Decalogue. Christ himself was vulnerable as an unborn child (Luke 1:31-35; Matthew 2:13-15), and his life was sought even before birth.
23: “But if there is serious injury, you are to take life for life.” The lex talionis principle applied to the most severe outcome. Christ's atonement operates on the same principle taken to its ultimate conclusion: “life for life” — his life for the life of his people. The proportional justice of the law finds its ultimate fulfillment in the cross, where the full weight of the law's demands is paid in full by the one whose life alone has infinite value.
24: “Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.” The lex talionis — proportional retaliation. Jesus explicitly overturns this as the governing principle of interpersonal ethics in Matthew 5:38-42: “You have heard it said, ‘An eye for an eye’... but I say to you, do not resist the one who is evil.” He transcends the lex talionis with non-retaliation and love of enemies. He himself received punishment disproportionate to his conduct so that we might receive mercy disproportionate to ours (2 Corinthians 5:21).
25: Burn for burn, wound for wound, bruise for bruise — the lex talionis applied to lesser injuries. Christ's body bore every category listed here: “by his wounds we are healed” (Isaiah 53:5; 1 Peter 2:24). His bruises (Isaiah 53:5: daka, crushed) fulfill the lex talionis at the cosmic level — the one who bore no guilt received every penalty the law prescribes, so that those who bear every guilt might receive every freedom the new covenant provides.
26: If a master destroys a slave's eye, the slave goes free for the loss of the eye. The slave's liberation as compensation for bodily harm acknowledges the slave as a person with inalienable physical dignity — the body of the slave has legal standing before YHWH. Christ sets every enslaved person free — not through bodily harm to himself serving as compensation (though he does bear bodily harm, Isaiah 50:6; Exodus 53:3-5) but through his death purchasing freedom for the enslaved (Colossians 1:13-14).
27: A knocked-out tooth earns the slave freedom. The smallest injury to the enslaved person's body triggers liberation. The principle is graduated mercy: the more the powerful damage the vulnerable, the greater the compensation owed. Christ's liberation of the damaged and broken — “he has sent me to bind up the brokenhearted, to proclaim liberty to the captives” (Isaiah 61:1; Luke 4:18) — takes the principle to its maximum: all the broken, all the captive, all who are damaged by sin go free through him.
28: A goring bull that kills a person must be stoned — the bull's meat not eaten. The principle: death caused by a dangerous animal is a judicial matter, not merely an accident. The extension of legal categories to animal behavior reflects the thoroughness of the covenant's reach into all of creation's order. Christ's domain extends over all things: he holds all things together (Colossians 1:17) and will bring all things under his feet (1 Corinthians 15:27; Ephesians 1:22).
29: If the bull was known to be dangerous and the owner was warned but did not pen it in, the owner is liable for execution (though he may pay a ransom). The owner's culpable negligence transfers the moral liability for the animal's violence to him. Responsible stewardship of the dangerous is a principle of the covenant law: those entrusted with power bear responsibility for its effects. Christ as the one who will judge the living and the dead (Acts 10:42; 2 Timothy 4:1) holds every steward accountable.
30: The ransom (kofer) that saves the owner's life — he may pay whatever is demanded as a ransom. The kofer (ransom, atonement) that substitutes for a life — the same root as kapporet (atonement cover) and kipper (atone). The ransom-principle in civil law is the type of the substitutionary atonement Christ provides: “The Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom (lytron) for many” (Matthew 20:28; Mark 10:45). His life is the ultimate kofer.
31: The same law applies if a bull gores a son or a daughter. Children are not second-class victims under the law; the same ransom-provision applies to them as to adults. Christ's redemption is comprehensive: “Let the little children come to me” (Mark 10:14) — the youngest and most vulnerable are equally included in the scope of his saving work (Matthew 18:2-5; Exodus 19:14).
32: If the bull gores a male or female slave, the owner pays thirty shekels of silver to the slave's master. Thirty shekels — the price of a slave (cf. Genesis 37:28). Zechariah 11:12-13 applies thirty pieces of silver to the rejected shepherd; Matthew 27:3-10 applies Zechariah 11:12-13 to the thirty pieces of silver paid for Jesus's betrayal. Christ was valued at the price of a slave — the ultimate inversion: the owner of all creation was sold for the price of the least valuable person in the ancient legal system.
33: The uncovered pit law — if someone digs or uncovers a pit and an animal falls in, they must compensate the owner. Responsibility for creating hazards — even inadvertent hazards — falls on the one who created them. The principle governs responsible action: known dangers must be guarded. Christ's warnings (Matthew 5:29-30; Exodus 18:6-9; Luke 17:1-2) about causing others to stumble reflect the same principle: those who create spiritual hazards bear liability before the ultimate Judge.
34: The pit-opener compensates the owner and takes the dead animal. The compensation principle: those who cause harm through negligence owe restitution to those harmed. Christ's redemption includes the restitution-principle: he restores what sin has destroyed, giving back more than was lost — “I will restore to you the years the locusts have eaten” (Joel 2:25; cf. Romans 5:20: “where sin abounded, grace abounded all the more”).
35: If one bull injures another and it dies, the owners split both the live animal and the proceeds from the dead one. The shared-loss principle distributes harm between parties when neither is fully negligent. The communal sharing of loss reflects the body-of-Christ principle: “if one part suffers, every part suffers with it” (1 Corinthians 12:26). Christ enters into human suffering as the supreme instance: he who caused no harm bears all the harm so that those who caused all the harm bear none of the penalty.
36: If the bull was known to be a gorer and the owner failed to pen it, he must replace the dead animal with a live one and take the dead for himself. The culpable owner must provide full replacement, not just partial compensation. The full-restitution principle underlies the atonement: Christ provides not a partial satisfaction but a complete replacement — not merely reducing the debt of sin but extinguishing it entirely (Hebrews 10:14: “by one sacrifice he has made perfect forever those who are being made holy”).
